BACKGROUND
RACIAL BREAK DOWN
RTC surveyed members who secured a summer internship in 2024,
collecting responses over three weeks via email and newsletter.
Individuals could participate in the survey regardless of their
internship role, company, or location. The survey was open for
approximately three weeks and there were 604 total respondents.
Data was analyzed to identify trends and insights while preserving
individual confidentiality.
Participants received a mix of multiple-choice, Likert-scale, and
open-ended questions on the following topics.

4 2024 Internship Experience Report

KEY INSIGHTS
Our analysis reveals three primary factors that are central to shaping an intern’s experience. Access to these factors can significantly impact
an intern’s willingness to accept a return offer, while also playing a role in the individual’s desire to continue pursuing a career in tech.
1 2 3
The Right Quality Comprehensive
Resources Mentorship Feedback
Read on, as we dive into the role of resources, the importance of mentorship, and the value of feedback.
5 2024 Internship Experience Report

Insights
01/ RESOURCES ARE CRITICAL
RESOURCES: THE IMPACT OF RESOURCE AVAILABILITY
ON RETURN OFFER ACCEPTANCE
Interns with access to structured resources, such as clear project
timelines, system tutorials, and shadowing or networking
opportunities, are significantly more likely to accept return offers.
For respondents who did not have access to any of the resources
surveyed, only 45% expressed a willingness to return to that
employer. With just one resource in place, the likelihood of a
return offer increases by at least 32%.
And, with all of the supports in place, 92% of respondents said
they would return for another internship.
The significance can’t be overstated: making all of these
resources available optimizes the intern experience.

MENTORSHIP: THE IMPACT OF MEETING FREQUENCY
ON RETURN OFFER ACCEPTANCE
Mentorship matters, and having even more
mentorship opportunities sets interns up for
success.
Out of those who reported having no mentors (and
no meetings), only 58% would accept a return
offer.
Interns who met with a mentor at least once per
week were 21 percentage points more likely to say
they would accept a return offer than those who
reported having no mentor.
Consistent, structured mentorship, which includes
meeting at least once a week, is key to keeping
interns engaged and invested in returning.

FEEDBACK: THE IMPACT OF DIVERSE FEEDBACK ON
RETURN OFFER ACCEPTANCE
Feedback Sources: Mentors, Managers, Team Members, Coworkers
COMPREHENSIVE FEEDBACK IS
03/
KEY IN PREDICTING THE
LIKELIHOOD OF RETURNING
TO A COMPANY
Respondents were asked if they had received regular feedback on
their work, and from whom.
Interns who receive regular feedback from numerous sources,
including mentors, managers, team members, and coworkers, are
more likely to return. Of those who received feedback from all four
sources, 94% said they would accept a return offer from the
organization and 83% reported feeling more certain about pursuing

REWRITING THE CODE PREPARES THE
NEXT GENERATION OF TECH TALENT TO
LEARN, INNOVATE, AND LEAD.
Investing in skill training, career readiness, and mentorship doesn’t
just support your employees, it strengthens your entire talent
pipeline. RTC makes it easier for you. Our community of over
35,000 students and early-career women in tech are actively
upskilling, networking, and preparing for their future in tech. In fact,
95% of RTC members feel certain about pursuing a career in tech
by graduation.
Why not make their next career goal a position at your company?
We are a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization dedicated to empowering
university students and early-career women in tech. Through
collaborative learning and career-focused development, we foster
belonging, champion opportunity, and equip future innovators with
the skills, knowledge, and connections to thrive—reshaping the
future of technology.
Meet our global community at rewritingthecode.org
